preFix.title: Proddy rug basics
preFix.subtitle: Pushing folded cloth strips through hessian backing with a prodder to build a dense, pile-like surface
preFix.excerpt: Proddy rugs (called proggy in the North of England) use a pointed prodder or awl to push short folded cloth strips through hessian from the front, leaving both ends protruding as a dense raised surface. Cut strips 2.5 cm wide by 7 to 10 cm long. No special equipment beyond the prodder and backing.
preFix.sourceNotes: Proddy rug making is a traditional Northern English domestic craft with deep roots in northeast England (Northumbria, Yorkshire) and the Northeast US. Technique described here is drawn from established guild instruction; not from a specific copyright source.

content.text: Proddy rug making is a Northern English domestic tradition known as
in Northumberland and County Durham. The process involves pushing short folded strips of cloth through a
or monks-cloth backing with a pointed
, leaving both tails protruding from the front to form a dense, slightly raised

is bound with rug binding tape to finish the edges. Historically made from repurposed fabric (old coats, blankets, worn clothing), proddy rugs are well suited to using textile off-cuts that are too thick to hook. The technique is worked with the right side of the backing facing up: you see the pattern forming as you work. A rug frame keeps the backing taut and prevents distortion.

items.name: Monks-cloth or 10-holes-per-10-cm hessian backing — cut to intended rug size plus 5 cm border on all sides for finishing
Cloth strips — cut from woven fabric (wool coating, denim, tweed, cotton canvas) 2.5 cm wide by 7 to 10 cm long
Prodder or sturdy awl — metal or bone-handled, pointed with a blunt enough tip not to shred fabric
Rug frame — to hold the backing taut while working

items.qty: perimeter of finished rug plus 10 cm
items.name: Rug binding tape — cotton or latex-backed, 2.5 cm wide
Rag rug strip cutter (optional) — speeds up cutting uniform strips from fabric lengths
Sharp craft scissors — for cutting strips and trimming the pile

content.text: Cut the backing fabric to the intended rug dimensions plus 5 cm on all sides. If using hessian, overlock or tape the raw edges immediately to prevent fraying during handling. Mount the backing onto the rug frame with the right side facing up. The backing should be taut but not stretched so tight that the weave holes distort. Mark the outer working border 5 cm in from the edge all around. This margin is left empty for binding.

Cut cloth strips 2.5 cm wide and 7 to 10 cm long. The exact length affects the pile height: 7 cm strips produce a shorter, denser pile; 10 cm strips a fuller, shaggier surface. Strips must be cut from woven fabric, not knitted. Knitted fabric stretches and will not sit in the backing. Wool coating, tweed, denim, and cotton canvas all work well. Cut on the straight grain of the fabric wherever possible. A rag rug strip cutter speeds this step considerably; cutting by scissors alone is time-consuming for large quantities.
Step 3: Fold and push the first strip

Hold one strip folded in half over the prodder tip, with both raw ends pointing upward. Push the prodder tip through a hole in the backing from the front (right side facing you), carrying the fold of the strip with it. The fold passes to the back of the backing; both raw ends remain protruding from the front. Remove the prodder from the fold, leaving the strip in place. The folded midpoint is now hidden behind the backing; the two tails are on the front.

Each strip occupies one backing hole. Leave one hole between each strip in every direction. One empty hole between strips gives a full, dense pile where the tails compress together. Leave two empty holes for a more open, shaggy pile. Begin at one corner of the design area and work in rows across the width, filling colour areas as designed. There is no fixed direction requirement; many makers work across rows, others fill by colour area. Either approach produces the same result.

Continue inserting folded strips across the working area, varying fabric colour and weight to build the design. Press each strip down firmly with a thumb after inserting to verify that the fold at the back is tight. A loose fold will let the strip pull free under use. Work systematically so no holes are missed; gaps in coverage leave thin patches in the pile. As the backing fills, the front surface will begin to look like a dense short-pile fabric.

When the design area is fully prodded, remove the rug from the frame. Turn the rug face-down. The reverse side shows a grid of fabric folds. Check that all folds lie in the same plane and that no strips have pulled through to protrude from the back. Pull any proud folds back to the front and re-push with the prodder.

Fold the 5 cm border of backing fabric over to the back of the rug, mitring the corners. Press the fold flat. Cut rug binding tape to the perimeter length of the rug plus 5 cm. Sew the binding tape over the folded edge using a running stitch or blanket stitch, catching the edge of the backing cloth and the reverse of the pile area. The binding tape both holds the border fold in place and protects the raw edge. At corners, fold the tape at 45 degrees and stitch the mitre. The finished selvedge should lie flat against the backing without puckering.

With the rug face up, run your palm over the surface to identify any strips protruding higher than their neighbours. Trim any outlying tails with sharp scissors to bring them level. For a more uniform surface, trim across the entire pile with scissors held flat, working in long even strokes. This is optional; many proddy makers leave the naturally uneven texture.

items.fix: Push the prodder deeper through the backing hole on each insertion so the fold clears 3 to 4 cm through to the back. Check each strip immediately after insertion by tugging gently from the front.
items.cause: The fold at the back of the backing is too shallow. Less than half the strip length passed through the hole.
items.problem: Strips pull out of the backing under normal handling

items.fix: Re-mount on the frame and tighten the lacing before continuing. Work insertion pressure evenly. Do not push the prodder at an angle.
items.cause: Uneven insertion tension pulling the backing out of shape, or the frame not holding the backing taut enough
items.problem: The backing distorts and waves while filling

items.fix: Hold the rug to the light to locate gaps. They show as translucent points in the backing. Insert additional strips into the empty holes.
items.cause: Some holes were skipped or strip spacing was inconsistent
items.problem: The pile looks sparse or uneven in places

items.fix: Remove the binding tape from the corner section, re-mitre the fabric fold at 45 degrees and press flat, then re-sew the binding tape over the mitred fold.
items.cause: Corner was not mitred before applying binding tape
items.problem: The binding tape wrinkles at the corners

tricolon: tricolon (three parallel items) — two adjectives almost always beats three
